summary: High-risk regulatory changes impacting food seasoning/snack compliance: A critical Codex labelling data extraction failed, requiring manual review. Allergen and Labelling updates are significant: US now includes sesame as a major allergen, with new rules for specific declarations (e.g., tree nut type, fish species), mandatory labeling for cochineal, carmine, FD&C Yellow No. 5, and sulfites (≥10 ppm), plus preventive controls for cross-contact. India mandates allergen declaration per FSSAI, while Taiwan reinforces mandatory labeling for 10 allergens and sulfites (≥10 mg/kg). GCC/GSO requires verification of new general labelling (GSO 9:2022) and mandatory shelf life (GSO 150-1:2013). Singapore emphasizes general labelling compliance and full ingredient/additive declaration. For Additive and Contaminant Limits: Singapore demands strict compliance for all additives/contaminants, prohibiting non-compliant products; Japan introduced a positive list for additives and prohibits toxic substances/pathogens; Thailand requires verification of permitted ingredients, contaminants (including new ASEAN MRLs by Feb 2026), toxins, and veterinary drug residues; and the US has new guidance/action levels for 3-MCPD (1 ppm) and total aflatoxins (20 ppb), alongside verification for prohibited additives. Microbiology updates include Japan prohibiting pathogen-contaminated food and Thailand requiring verification of pathogenic microorganism standards.

Prohibited substances are listed in FDA regulations (21 CFR Part 189). Delisted color additives are in FDA regulations (21 CFR 81.10 and/or 81.30). Verification against the inventory is required.
The 9 major food allergens recognized by the U.S. are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame.
Guidance level for 3-MCPD in acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces.
Action level for total aflatoxins in general foods.
Action level for total aflatoxins in peanuts and peanut products.

The CGMP & PC rule (21 CFR part 117) establishes requirements for allergen preventive controls to prevent allergen cross-contact and undeclared allergens. Facilities must put written procedures in place.
Cochineal extract and carmine must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.
All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 must identify it on their labels.
The FDA issued a final rule defining “gluten-free” for food labeling. Compliance requirements for fermented and hydrolyzed foods bearing the “gluten-free” claim were issued on August 12, 2020.

Food labels must identify the food source of all major food allergens. This can be done in parentheses following the ingredient name (e.g., 'lecithin (soy)') or in a 'contains' statement immediately after or next to the ingredient list (e.g., 'Contains wheat, milk, and soy.').

changes.parameter: Specific Allergen Declaration (Tree Nuts, Fish, Crustacean Shellfish)
changes.notes: The specific type of tree nut (e.g., almonds, pecans, walnuts), and the species of fish (e.g., bass, flounder, cod) and Crustacean shellfish (e.g., crab, lobster, shrimp) must be declared.
Various sulfiting agents must be declared on food labels when present in food at a concentration of ≥10 parts per million total sulfites.
